original_abstract: Minority populations remain significantly underrepresented in clinical research, a disparity that obscures the understanding of chronic diseases and limits the application of precision medicine in high-risk communities. This study explores the multifaceted barriers to recruitment in the United States, including historical mistrust, socioeconomic pressures, and linguistic obstacles. Drawing on decades of global experience in infectious disease research, the authors present a successful community-centric model implemented in the Texas Rio Grande Valley. Since 2004, this approach has utilized a team of locally recruited and trained staff—bilingual community members who share the culture of the participants—to enroll and retain a longitudinal cohort of over 5,000 Mexican Americans. By prioritizing trust, reducing participant burden through localized outreach, and providing feedback on research findings, this model has achieved high levels of engagement in complex multi-omic and clinical studies. The findings suggest that bridging the recruitment gap requires investigators to move beyond "parachute science" and invest in community infrastructure, transforming research into a collaborative partnership that benefits both scientific discovery and public health equity.
humanized_abstract: Minority populations are disproportionately underrepresented in most clinical studies. As a result, there is a paucity of information regarding chronic diseases that are of particular relevance to minority and other high-risk populations and precision medicine is not applicable. We identified potential reasons for underrepresentation including past mistrust, socioeconomic constraints, and language barriers. Based on our vast international experience with infectious disease studies in the last 30 years, we developed a community-based approach demonstrated with evidence obtained in the Texas Rio Grande Valley (TRGV) in the southern tip of Texas. This valley borders Mexico and is predominantly Hispanic. Our team has been conducting a longitudinal cohort study of Mexican Americans since 2004 by utilizing a community-based approach that we developed over the past 30 years conducting international studies in developing countries. The approach is based on the use of community recruited and trained members of the target population to enroll and be part of the research process. In this case, the population to be studied is the Hispanic population of the TRGV. The approach utilizes bilingual members of the same culture as the population under study. These members are recruited from the community and are trained to be outreach personnel and participants in the proposed research study. The goal of this approach is to enhance trust, simplify and decrease burden to the research participants, and to return results of the research to the community to allow participants to obtain benefits from knowledge derived from their participation. Our approach has been highly successful in obtaining participant recruitment for large multi-omic and clinical studies. The results indicate that increasing minority participation in research conducted in the US will require much more than just "tinkering" with current practices and that this challenge will only be met if investigators invest in the development of community-based research resources to foster a successful and mutually beneficial partnership between investigators and the communities they wish to study in order to enhance health equity and research advances for the benefit of all.
